frameworks.name: Bayes factors
frameworks.one_line: Relative evidence for one theory over another from the data.

frameworks.name: Power analysis
frameworks.one_line: Pre-data sensitivity check argued to be insufficient for null-result interpretation.

frameworks.name: Confidence intervals
frameworks.one_line: Interval estimation considered as an alternative way to read non-significant results.

frameworks.name: Null hypothesis significance testing
frameworks.one_line: Frequentist framework whose non-significant outcomes motivate the problem.

core_question: How should researchers decide whether a non-significant result counts against a theory rather than reflecting data insensitivity?
key_assumptions: A theory can be specified well enough to make a quantitative prediction
Evidence is meaningful only relative to at least two competing hypotheses
Data themselves can index their own sensitivity
key_findings: Non-significant results do not automatically license any scientific conclusion
Power, intervals, and Bayes factors give different leverage on null findings
Bayes factors use the data to gauge their own sensitivity in distinguishing theories
interpretations: Bayes factors coherently distinguish evidence for the null from data insensitivity
Bayes factors better link theory to data than power or intervals for null results
